title: Psalms 66
subtitle: Shout for Joy to God, All the Earth

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — “Come and See What God Has Done”

heb.paragraph: "How awesome (nōrāʾ) are your deeds! Come and see what God has done, his awesome deeds for mankind!" (vv.3, 5). Universal summons to praise (vv.1–4). Remember the exodus: he turned the sea into dry land, they passed through waters on foot (v.6). God tested and refined like silver (vv.10–12). "We went through fire and water, but you brought us to a place of abundance" (v.12).

notes.note: "Shout for joy to God, all the earth! How awesome are your deeds!" Universal praise. All the earth, not just Israel.
"He turned the sea into dry land, they passed through the waters on foot." The exodus memory: the defining act of God. Every psalm of praise returns to this.

"You tested us; you refined us like silver. We went through fire and water, but you brought us to a place of abundance." Testing produces purity. Suffering produces abundance. The route to plenty goes through fire and water.

refs.note: The song of Moses and the Lamb with 'all nations will worship' fulfills the psalm's universal praise vision.
Every tongue confessing Christ echoes the psalm's all-earth acclamation.
The Red Sea crossing explicitly referenced in the psalm as paradigm of divine deliverance.

hist.context: Psalm 66 combines communal hymn (vv.1-12) with individual thanksgiving (vv.13-20). The reference to passing through sea and river (v.6) recalls both the Red Sea and Jordan crossings. The 'testing' and 'refining' imagery (vv.10-12) interprets national suffering as divine purification. The shift to first-person singular in verse 13 suggests the psalm was adapted for personal use.

sections.header: Verses 13–20 — “God Has Not Rejected My Prayer”

heb.paragraph: "Praise be to God, who has not rejected my prayer (təfillâ) or withheld his love from me!" (v.20). Individual thanksgiving follows communal praise. David comes with burnt offerings (v.13), fulfils his vows (v.14), offers sacrifices (v.15). The confession: "If I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened" (v.18). Prayer’s prerequisite: a clean heart.

notes.note: "Come and hear, all you who fear God; let me tell you what he has done for me. I cried out to him with my mouth; his praise was on my tongue." Testimony: personal experience shared publicly.
"If I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened." The condition for answered prayer: unconfessed sin blocks the channel.
"God has not rejected my prayer or withheld his love." The testimony’s conclusion: prayer heard, love received.

refs.note: The sacrifice of praise through lips confessing God's name fulfills the psalm's vow to bring offerings.
Confidence before God and receiving what we ask echoes the psalm's answered-prayer testimony.
The prayer of a righteous person being powerful connects to the psalm's statement that God listens to the upright.

hist.context: The individual section (vv.13-20) describes fulfilling vows made during crisis — burnt offerings of rams, bulls, and goats represent substantial sacrifice. The confession 'if I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened' (v.18) links moral integrity to prayer's effectiveness. The psalm models public testimony following private deliverance.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: The awesome God whose deeds compel universal praise (vv.1-7,10-12,16-20)
ppl.text: The psalm opens with universal summons: 'Shout for joy to God, all the earth!' (v.1). God's 'awesome deeds' include turning 'the sea into dry land' (v.6, the Exodus) — this historical act becomes evidence for all nations. God 'rules forever by his power, his eyes watch the nations' (v.7). The testing imagery is metallurgical: 'You refined us like silver… we went through fire and water' (vv.10-12), suggesting suffering as purification, not punishment. The psalm pivots from corporate (vv.1-12) to personal testimony (vv.13-20).

ppl.name: The Psalmist (Individual Worshiper)
ppl.role: One who testifies to answered prayer and fulfilled vows (vv.13-20)
ppl.text: The shift from 'we' to 'I' at verse 13 moves the psalm from national to personal: 'I will come to your temple with burnt offerings and fulfill my vows' (v.13). The theological insight of v.18 is critical: 'If I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened.' Answered prayer confirms the worshiper's moral integrity — not perfection, but sincerity. This connection between heart-condition and prayer-efficacy runs through James 5:16 and 1 John 3:21-22.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVCome and see what God has done, his awesome deeds for mankind!ESVCome and see what God has done: he is awesome in his deeds toward the children of man.NIVHe rules forever by his power, his eyes watch the nations— let not the rebellious rise up against him.ESVwho rules by his might forever, whose eyes keep watch on the nations\u2014 let not the rebellious exalt themselves. Selah

rec.text: ""Shout for joy to God, all the earth! How awesome are your deeds!" Universal praise. All the earth, not just Israel."

rows.label: Come and Hear — 66:13–20
rows.text: "Let me tell you what he has done." If I cherished sin, he wouldn’t listen. "He has not rejected my prayer."

hebtext.note: "How awesome (nōrāʾ) are your deeds! Come and see what God has done, his awesome deeds for mankind!" (vv.3, 5). Universal summons to praise (vv.1–4). Remember the exodus: he turned the sea into dry land, they passed through waters on foot (v.6). God tested and refined like silver (vv.10–12). "We went through fire and water, but you brought us to a place of abundance" (v.12).

"Praise be to God, who has not rejected my prayer (təfillâ) or withheld his love from me!" (v.20). Individual thanksgiving follows communal praise. David comes with burnt offerings (v.13), fulfils his vows (v.14), offers sacrifices (v.15). The confession: "If I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened" (v.18). Prayer’s prerequisite: a clean heart.

themes.note: Worship peaks: universal summons + personal testimony. Holiness: cherished sin blocks prayer. Mission: all the earth summoned.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on 'Shout for joy to God, all the earth,' consider asking God whether your joy in Him is expansive enough to include all peoples — or whether you have limited His praise to your own circle.
